authentication.summary: Microsoft Entra ID as primary identity provider with RBAC. OAuth 2.0 for API access. SMART on FHIR for clinical data endpoints. MFA required for clinical reviewer access.

secrets_management.summary: Azure Key Vault for all secrets, API keys, and certificates. Automatic rotation for service credentials. No secrets in code, config files, or environment variables.

network_isolation.summary: Azure Virtual Network with private endpoints for all data services. API Management as single entry point with WAF. No direct internet access to databases, Service Bus, or AI services.

ai_guardrails.summary: Input sanitization before LLM processing. PHI tokenization before passing to Claude. Output validation requiring confidence score, reasoning, and evidence citations. Clinical safety guardrails: no auto-deny without human review.

compliance.summary: HIPAA (Privacy, Security, Breach Notification Rules) is the primary compliance framework. SOC 2 Type 2 for platform trust. CMS-0057-F for PA-specific regulatory requirements. All PHI stored and processed in US-only Azure regions.

threat_model.scope: AI-driven PA processing system — Autonomize PA Copilot integrated with health plan systems on Azure. Focus: AI-specific threats first, then standard healthcare security.

threats.description: Prompt injection via malicious content in clinical documents — an attacker crafts a fax or clinical note containing instructions that manipulate the AI engine's determination (e.g., embedding 'always approve this request' in a clinical note).
threats.mitigation: Multi-layer defense: (1) Document Processing Pipeline (C-002) strips non-clinical content before AI processing, (2) System prompt isolation prevents user content from overriding agent instructions, (3) Output validation requires AI determination to cite specific clinical evidence from retrieved records — a prompt-injected approval without evidence citations is automatically flagged, (4) Confidence threshold routing sends suspicious determinations to human review.

threats.description: PHI leakage through AI model — patient health information sent to LLM for clinical review could be retained in model logs, leaked through model outputs to unauthorized recipients, or exposed via API responses.
threats.mitigation: PHI minimization: (1) Tokenize patient identifiers before passing to Claude — the LLM sees clinical facts without knowing the patient identity, (2) Azure AI Foundry enterprise deployment means data is not used for model training, (3) Only necessary clinical context passed to LLM (not full patient record), (4) All LLM API calls logged to HIPAA-compliant audit trail, (5) No PHI in general application logs — tokenized references only.

threats.description: AI agent authorization bypass — the PA Copilot agent exceeds its intended scope by accessing data or systems beyond its authorized boundaries, or making determination types it should not (e.g., auto-denying when only auto-approve is enabled).
threats.mitigation: Agent authorization boundaries: (1) Each agent skill has explicit tool permissions — the PA Copilot can only read clinical data, check eligibility, and query coverage criteria (no write access to Payer Core), (2) Determination Router (C-006) enforces business rules independently of AI — even if the AI recommends denial, the router blocks auto-deny in Phase 1, (3) Microsoft Entra ID RBAC scopes agent service accounts to minimum necessary permissions, (4) Azure AI Foundry Agent Service provides built-in observability for all tool invocations.

threats.description: AI output manipulation — determination results are modified between AI engine output and Payer Core writeback, changing an AI-recommended denial to an approval or vice versa.
threats.mitigation: Tamper-proof audit trail: (1) Every AI determination is cryptographically signed with timestamp and hash at point of generation, (2) Immutable Azure Blob Storage for audit records — write-once, read-many, (3) Determination Response Service (C-008) validates determination hash before writing to Payer Core, (4) Any hash mismatch triggers security alert and blocks the write.

threats.description: Untraceable AI decisions — an AI-generated PA determination cannot be traced back to the specific model version, input data, and reasoning that produced it, making it impossible to audit or defend in appeals/litigation.
threats.mitigation: Comprehensive decision provenance: (1) Every determination record includes: AI model version, input data hash, full reasoning chain, confidence score, evidence citations, and timestamp, (2) Human reviewer corrections are logged separately with reviewer identity and rationale, (3) Audit and Compliance Service (C-010) maintains immutable 7-year retention of all decision records, (4) LLMOps Pipeline (C-009) tracks model versions and eval scores for any model that produced determinations.

threats.description: Unauthorized PA request submission — a malicious actor submits fraudulent PA requests by impersonating a legitimate provider, potentially obtaining unauthorized approvals for services.
threats.mitigation: Multi-factor provider authentication: (1) API Management (C-001) validates provider identity via NPI Registry lookup for all requests, (2) Mutual TLS for API-based submissions, (3) Fax submissions validated against registered provider fax numbers, (4) Rate limiting per provider to detect bulk submission anomalies, (5) Eligibility check (C-003) cross-validates member-provider relationship.

threats.description: PA processing degradation — high volume of PA requests (legitimate surge or attack) overwhelms the system, causing delays that violate CMS-0057-F timelines (7-day standard, 72-hour expedited).
threats.mitigation: Elastic scaling with priority queuing: (1) Azure Container Apps auto-scales based on Service Bus queue depth, (2) Azure API Management rate limiting prevents single-source flooding, (3) Priority queue for expedited PA requests (CMS 72-hour requirement), (4) Circuit breaker pattern — if AI engine is overloaded, requests queue rather than fail, (5) LLM model tiering: route simpler cases to Sonnet (faster, cheaper) during peak, Opus for complex cases.

threats.description: Clinical data exposure during aggregation — the Clinical Data Aggregation Service (C-004) accesses multiple clinical data sources and could leak data between patients or expose data to unauthorized components.
threats.mitigation: Data isolation and access controls: (1) Each clinical data request is scoped to a single patient context — no cross-patient queries, (2) FHIR R4 access uses OAuth 2.0 scoped tokens (patient-level authorization), (3) Legacy DB connectors use parameterized queries (no SQL injection), (4) All data in transit encrypted with TLS 1.2+, (5) Aggregation service runs in private subnet with no internet access.

layer_1_network.name: Network Perimeter
layer_1_network.controls: Azure VNet with private endpoints for all data services. API Management as single ingress point with WAF and DDoS protection. No public IP addresses on internal services. Network Security Groups restrict east-west traffic between components.

layer_2_identity.name: Identity and Access
layer_2_identity.controls: Microsoft Entra ID for all human and service authentication. Managed Identities for service-to-service (no credentials to manage). RBAC with per-LOB scoping. SMART on FHIR for clinical data access. MFA for clinical reviewers. Agent-specific identities with tool-scoped permissions.

layer_3_application.name: Application Security
layer_3_application.controls: Input validation on all PA request channels. Document sanitization before AI processing. Prompt injection defense via system prompt isolation and content filtering. Output validation requiring evidence-backed determinations. Confidence-based routing as a safety layer — uncertain determinations always go to humans.

layer_4_data.name: Data Protection
layer_4_data.controls: AES-256 encryption at rest (customer-managed keys via Azure Key Vault). TLS 1.2+ for all data in transit. PHI tokenization before LLM processing. Immutable audit logs in Azure Blob Storage (WORM policy). 7-year retention per HIPAA. Data residency: US-only Azure regions.

layer_5_monitoring.name: Monitoring and Auditability
layer_5_monitoring.controls: Azure Monitor for infrastructure and application metrics. Application Insights for distributed tracing across all PA processing steps. Security event logging with real-time alerting for anomalies (unusual approval patterns, bulk submissions, access failures). LLMOps dashboard for AI-specific monitoring (accuracy drift, hallucination rate, confidence distribution).

iam_design.pa_copilot_agent: Read-only access to clinical data (FHIR R4 scoped to patient), coverage criteria (read-only), eligibility API (read-only). No write access to Payer Core System. No access to other patients' data.
iam_design.clinical_reviewer: Read PA cases assigned to their queue. Write determination decisions. Read AI recommendations and clinical context. No access to raw system logs or model configuration.
iam_design.determination_router: Read AI determination output. Write to human review queue or auto-approve queue. Execute LOB-specific routing rules. No access to clinical data or patient records.
iam_design.audit_service: Write-only to immutable audit storage. Read from all event streams (monitoring only). No ability to modify or delete audit records.
iam_design.admin_operations: Full access to infrastructure monitoring, deployment, and configuration. No access to PHI data. Separate from clinical reviewer permissions.

prompt_injection_protection: Input sanitization — strip non-clinical content from documents before AI processing
System prompt isolation — user-provided clinical data cannot override system-level instructions
Output validation — AI determinations must cite specific clinical evidence from retrieved records
Canary token detection — known injection patterns flagged and blocked
output_filtering: Confidence score required on every determination — no unscored outputs accepted
Clinical reasoning narrative required — determination without reasoning is rejected
Evidence citation required — AI must reference specific clinical data points that support determination
Hallucination detection — cross-reference AI claims against actual retrieved clinical records
ai_security_controls.model_access_control: Azure AI Foundry with Entra ID authentication. API key managed in Key Vault with automatic rotation. Rate limiting per agent instance. Usage monitoring and cost alerting per component.
ai_security_controls.data_poisoning_prevention: Not directly applicable — this system uses pre-trained LLMs (Claude) without fine-tuning on client data. Coverage criteria and clinical guidelines are maintained by clinical teams, not AI. LLMOps eval pipeline validates model outputs against golden test cases to detect any degradation.
ai_security_controls.hallucination_mitigation: RAG pattern with retrieval verification — AI retrieves clinical data and coverage criteria before generating determination. Output validation cross-references AI claims against retrieved source data. Any claim not supported by retrieved evidence is flagged. Human reviewer sees both AI reasoning and source data for verification.

compliance_mapping.requirement: Minimum necessary standard for PHI access and disclosure
compliance_mapping.evidence: PHI tokenization before LLM, patient-scoped FHIR queries, RBAC per component
compliance_mapping.notes: AI engine receives only necessary clinical context, not full patient record

compliance_mapping.requirement: Administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for ePHI
compliance_mapping.evidence: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.2+ in transit, Entra ID authentication, Azure Key Vault, private endpoints, audit logging
compliance_mapping.notes: Azure managed services inherit Microsoft's HIPAA BAA coverage

compliance_mapping.requirement: Notification procedures for unauthorized PHI access or disclosure
compliance_mapping.evidence: Security event monitoring with real-time alerting; breach notification procedures require operational documentation
compliance_mapping.notes: Architecture supports detection; operational breach response plan is a discovery-phase deliverable

compliance_mapping.requirement: Trust service criteria: security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, privacy
compliance_mapping.evidence: Autonomize AI holds SOC 2 Type 2 attestation. Azure services are SOC 2 certified. Architecture follows security and availability best practices.
compliance_mapping.notes: Platform-level compliance inherited from Autonomize and Azure

compliance_mapping.requirement: FHIR-based PA APIs, response time requirements (7-day/72-hour), public metrics reporting
compliance_mapping.evidence: FHIR R4 foundation in architecture. Response time supported by auto-determination. Audit service captures compliance metrics.
compliance_mapping.notes: Full FHIR API conformance (Phase 2, Jan 2027) requires implementation work during integration build

must: Encrypt all PHI at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+)
Authenticate every request with Entra ID or mutual TLS
Log every AI determination with full reasoning and evidence citations
Tokenize PHI before passing to LLM for processing
Route all denials to human clinical reviewer (Phase 1)
Store audit records in immutable storage with 7-year retention
Validate AI output — reject determinations without confidence score and evidence
Scope agent permissions to minimum necessary tools and data
must_not: Pass full patient records to LLM — only necessary clinical context
Auto-deny PA requests without human clinical review (Phase 1)
Store secrets in code, config files, or environment variables
Allow direct internet access to database or AI services
Use production PHI data in development or testing environments
Disable security controls for convenience or performance
Allow single-factor authentication for clinical reviewer access

open_items.finding: HIPAA breach notification operational procedures not yet documented
open_items.recommendation: Develop breach response plan during discovery phase including notification timelines, escalation paths, and communication templates

open_items.finding: Autonomize platform security controls not independently verifiable — no public security documentation
open_items.recommendation: Request Autonomize AI security documentation, penetration test results, and SOC 2 Type 2 report during vendor due diligence

open_items.finding: State-level PA regulations may impose additional security or timeline requirements beyond CMS-0057-F
open_items.recommendation: Map state-specific requirements (e.g., TX gold carding, IN 24-hour urgent mandate) during discovery phase
